title: Regulatory Fragmentation
abstract: Regulatory fragmentation occurs when multiple federal agencies oversee a single issue. Using the full text of the Federal Register, the government's official daily publication, we provide the first systematic evidence on the extent and costs of regulatory fragmentation. Fragmentation increases the firm's costs while lowering its productivity, profitability, and growth. Moreover, it deters entry into an industry and increases the propensity of small firms to exit. These effects arise from redundancy and, more prominently, from inconsistencies between government agencies. Our results uncover a new source of regulatory burden, and we show that agency costs among regulators contribute to this burden.

article.introduction:
The federal government is themost influential actor in the U.S. economy, and many studies document the significant burden imposed by the government on companies. The literature tends to focus on one aspect of this burden: the cost of complying with specific regulations. In this paper, we highlight a different notion of burden, namely, regulatory fragmentation: the regulation of a single topic by multiple federal agencies. As of September 2021, the Government Accountability Office (GAO) has issued 11 annual reports on the fragmentation, overlap, and duplication of federal activities (GAO (2021)). Policy makers have debated how to eliminate some of the regulatory fragmentation (GAO (2016)), and the Business Roundtable more recently warned that fragmentation “poses significant challenges to American businesses and can dampen economic activity across the wider U.S. economy” (Business Roundtable (2019)). Our paper offers the first systematic research on regulatory fragmentation and its economic impact on companies.
Our analysis is based on novel data from the Federal Register (FR), the official daily publication of the U.S. federal government. All federal agencies publish comprehensive accounts of their activities in the FR, including drafts of new regulations and enforcement actions. As such, the FR offers a unique opportunity to identify the topics supervised by each agency. We download the full text of the FR's daily editions since 1994 and use a machine learning technique known as latent Dirichlet analysis (LDA) to categorize the government's activities into 100 topics. We then calculate the fragmentation of each topic across federal agencies. The key takeaway is that the regulation of many topics is fragmented across multiple government agencies.
Ex ante, it is not clear how regulatory fragmentation might affect companies. On the one hand, companies could benefit from oversight by multiple agencies. First, fragmentation may increase companies’ ability to capture regulators. In the early stages of regulation, firms can focus on the agency that they can best influence. After final rules are set, firms can choose to follow the least restrictive rules, analogous to firms benefiting from choice of court venue (see, for example, Bebchuk and Hamdani (2002)). Second, in a world with regulatory competition, these factors could contribute to more efficient regulation, or alternatively to a race to the bottom among regulators, in either case benefitting firms.
On the other hand, regulatory fragmentation could be costly for firms. The involvement of multiple agencies can lead to duplicative requirements that increase firm compliance costs. Even worse, it may lead to regulatory inconsistency that creates uncertainty with respect to which regulations the firm must satisfy. Since the firm cannot fully anticipate new regulatory decisions, its ability to plan is debilitated. Finally, fragmentation may decrease firms’ ability to capture regulators, an additional factor that can contribute to higher costs stemming from regulation.1
The two hypotheses lead to opposite predictions. If regulatory fragmentation is beneficial, it should reduce costs while increasing profitability, productivity, and growth. Alternatively, if fragmentation is costly, it should lead to higher costs and to lower profitability, productivity, and growth. To test our predictions, we develop a firm-specific measure of regulatory fragmentation. In addition to accounting for the fragmentation of each topic across federal agencies, our measure accounts for the relevance of each topic for each firm. We formalize this intuition as follows. First, as mentioned above, we measure the fragmentation of each topic across government agencies using textual analysis of the FR. Second, we compute the importance of each topic for each firm×$ \times $year observation as the fraction of words in the firm's annual report devoted to the topic. Finally, we multiply the fragmentation of the topic by its relative importance for each firm and sum the products across all 100 topics.2
To validate our measure, we identify FR documents that explicitly mention public firms by name (using data from Chen and Kalmenovitz (2024)). We find that firms with high fragmentation are mentioned by significantly more agencies. In addition, we search the firms’ annual reports for explicit mentions of federal agencies. Within the finance industry, where there is a greater propensity to mention regulating agencies, firms with high fragmentation mention significantly more agencies. Taken together, these tests provide independent verification of our fragmentation measure.3
Our measure varies both in the cross-section and within-firm over time. In the time series, regulatory fragmentation has decreased for most firms, consistent with efforts by policy makers across the government (GAO (2021)). However, there is heterogeneity in this trend. As an example, the topic “Grants: Housing and Community Development” has become increasingly fragmented across agencies, and at the same time the regulatory fragmentation of the firm Sunrise Assisted Living (which is primarily engaged in housing projects) has increased. In the cross-section, inter-industry patterns are insightful. For example, financial companies operate under greater fragmentation than many other industries: While financial companies concentrate on a relatively small number of topics, those topics tend to be regulated by multiple federal agencies.
A broad set of empirical tests indicates that regulatory fragmentation is detrimental to firms. Companies with higher fragmentation have higher sales, general, and administrative (SG&A) costs, but exhibit lower total factor productivity (TFP) and profitability (return on assets). We conjecture that if regulatory fragmentation consumes company resources and leads fewer projects to have positive net present value (NPV), firm growth should slow. Consistent with this view, we find that fragmentation reduces both sales growth and asset growth. We further find evidence consistent with the high costs of fragmentation deterring entry into an industry and pushing out smaller incumbent firms. In sum, consistent with points raised by the GAO and Business Roundtable, our empirical analyses support the hypothesis that regulatory fragmentation is costly.
Our findings are robust to a large set of controls and saturated fixed-effects specifications. To disentangle regulatory fragmentation from the sheer quantity of regulation, we proxy for the latter using the number of words in the FR devoted to each regulatory topic.4To disentangle the fragmentation of each regulatory topic (across government agencies) from the effects of companies operating across many areas, we control for firm size (total assets), number of business segments, and the dispersion of topics discussed in the firm's 10-K. To account for the possibility that distressed firms draw the attention of multiple regulators, we control for known determinants of investment opportunities (Tobin's Q and cash flows). To address the concern that high fragmentation may be correlated with restructuring or expansion into new product markets, we remove firms whose assets changed by more than 20% in absolute value and firms that moved into a new industry (based on Hoberg and Phillips (2016)). Finally, we also include firm and year×$ \times $industry fixed effects, removing industry trends and comparing companies from the same industry that face different levels of fragmentation. The highly significant results suggest that these effects occur at the firm level and are identified within-firm over time.
Having established that regulatory fragmentation is costly to firms, we examine the potential underlying channels: redundancy, inconsistency, and regulatory capture. To evaluate redundancy versus inconsistency, we identify FR documents that are co-authored by multiple agencies versus documents that are solo-authored by a single agency. Co-authored documents are clearly coordinated and therefore unlikely to yield inconsistent regulatory requirements. Indeed, we find that all of the effects reported above are weaker when agencies work together. Put differently, the burden of fragmentation is higher when it yields inconsistent—as opposed to merely duplicative—requirements. To evaluate the relation between fragmentation and regulatory capture, we analyze patterns in firm lobbying. We find that higher fragmentation is associated with significantly lower lobbying, consistent with firms perceiving the benefits of lobbying to be lower when it is less clear where to focus their efforts. These findings suggest that higher fragmentation contributes to less regulatory capture, which leads in turn to higher compliance costs for firms (albeit potential societal benefits).
Our measure of fragmentation uniquely captures the total flow of regulatory activity, rather than just the stock of existing rules. Importantly, this flow captures the early stages of new rulemaking, the final passing of new rules, and the ongoing requirements of existing rules. We take advantage of this breadth of data to address several follow-on questions. First, we contrast the effects across different stages of regulatory activity. Although academic studies focus primarily on final rules, they represent only 32% of total government activity, as captured in the FR. The earlier stages of rulemaking account for an additional 24%, and the remaining 44% includes actions such as hearings on issues regarding potential future rulemaking, transaction approvals, and enforcement actions (all classified as “notices”). We find that the negative effects of fragmentation exist in each stage of regulatory activity. Second, we contrast the fragmentation across new rules versus rule modifications. Consistent with both stages of regulatory activity consuming firm resources, we find that both have similar significant negative effects on firms. Finally, as discussed above, we focus on a flow-based measure of regulatory fragmentation. While a full analysis of the entire stock of regulation in existence at each point in time, and the associated enforcement of this body of regulation, is beyond the scope of this paper, we form an alternative stock-based measure of fragmentation. That is based on the weighted average of the past 5 or 10 years of regulatory activity in the FR, where more recent years are weighted more heavily. The results are similar to our main results. In aggregate, our findings demonstrate how firms are influenced by the fragmentation across different types of regulatory activity: Firms incur costs to stay abreast of upcoming regulatory activity, to comply with new regulations, and to adapt to changes in existing regulations.
In the last set of analyses, we consider which factors contribute to regulatory fragmentation. One factor is regulators’ efforts to serve the public interest. This can lead to redundancy between agencies to ensure compliance, or to inconsistency if an agency determines that another agency's regulations are insufficient or misguided. A second factor is unintentional actions among regulators. Given the sheer size of the government, actors within one agency may be unaware of existing regulation coming out of other agencies. Third, agency issues may play a role, for example, if empire building and regulatory schooling effects incentivize regulators to extend their rulemaking activities outside the agency's core area. While we cannot definitively disentangle all of these factors, we empirically examine the influence of agency costs using individual data on the compensation and ranks of all federal employees. We find that agency activity is positively related to promotions, indicating that employees are rewarded for increasing their agency's footprint in ways that are consistent with empire building. The effect is particularly strong when the agency ventures into topics that are outside of its core expertise, which contributes to the overall fragmentation.
We note that across all of our analyses, we lack an exogenous shock that randomly affects regulatory fragmentation across firms. While we have conducted numerous analyses to mitigate endogeneity concerns, we nonetheless acknowledge this as a limitation. The lack of such exogenous shocks is common in the regulation literature, and it represents a challenge for all future research in this area.
There is growing recognition of the need to better understand how regulation in all of its forms affects economic activity. Against this backdrop, our paper makes two contributions. First, current studies focus on the number of rules (La Porta et al. (1998), Bandiera et al. (2000), Botero et al. (2004), Mulligan and Shleifer (2005), Djankov et al. (2006)), the length of regulatory text (Dawson and Seater (2013), Al-Ubaydli and McLaughlin (2017)), and the estimated costs of compliance (Calomiris et al. (2020), Kalmenovitz (2023), Simkovic et al. (2023)). In this paper, we highlight a largely unknown aspect of regulatory burden, namely, the “industrial organization” of the federal government. We show that the burden borne by companies depends on not only the sheer quantity of regulations, but also the number of agencies that are involved in the design and implementation of those regulations. Our measure employs public data to capture the full range of the federal government's activities, and thus offers a comprehensive and replicable measure of the federal government's burden.
Second, we add to the literature on the real impact of regulation. Classic theories on this subject range from public interest (Pigou (1938), Demsetz (1974), Joskow and Rose (1989)) to public choice (Tullock (1967), Stigler (1971), Krueger (1974), Posner (1974), Peltzman (1976), Baumol (1983), Becker (1983), Olson (2009)). Our results emphasize how one important aspect of the government's outreach imposes significant costs on companies, both real and financial: When more government agencies seek to regulate a given topic, companies experience more negative outcomes. Overall, our results are more consistent with public choice theories, which highlight negative externalities from the regulatory process. While our findings should not be interpreted as conclusive evidence on the efficacy of federal regulations, they offer a fresh perspective on the nature of regulatory burden and its economic consequences. Our findings also open the door for future work on the effects of fragmentation, for example, fragmentation in the implementation of regulations via enforcement actions.
The remainder of the paper proceeds as follows. SectionIdescribes the data and our sample. SectionIIoutlines our empirical strategy, including the formation of our main regulatory fragmentation variable. This section also presents validation tests and provides descriptive statistics. In SectionIII, we describe the evolution of government activity over time and present a case study. In SectionIV, we examine the effects of regulatory fragmentation on economic activity, including both firm- and industry-level effects. In SectionV, we investigate the sources of burden arising from regulatory fragmentation, for example, regulatory inconsistency and regulatory capture. In SectionVI, we discuss the potential determinants of regulatory fragmentation, and we empirically examine the extent to which agency factors play a role. Finally, SectionVIIconcludes, AppendixApresents the list of agencies, and AppendixBdescribes the variables.
article.1 Data:
We rely on the FR as our primary source of government activities. Often described as the daily journal of the federal government, the FR is published every business day.5The FR is divided into four sections:Noticesinclude announcements such as hearings and public meetings, grant applications, administrative orders, and enforcements;6Proposed Rulesinclude petitions for rulemaking, drafts of pending regulations, and other advanced proposals;Rules and Regulationsinclude the final version of rules and policy statements and interpretations of existing rules; andPresidential Documentsinclude executive orders and proclamations.
Each federal agency is required to publish designated documents in the FR. This includes activity related to previously passed rules (e.g., details regarding rule implementation, ongoing requirements of existing rules, and company-specific actions), activity unrelated to rules (e.g., grant applications and hearings), and activity related to new rulemaking. With respect to new rulemaking, this includes at a minimum the proposed rule and the final version of the rule, but it often also includes notices of meetings and solicitation of comments. When the rulemaking process is finished, the new regulation is incorporated into the CFR.
Consistent with the FR representing one step in the rulemaking process that leads to revisions to the CFR, these two sources are linked to each other. First, every proposed rule and final rule in the FR denotes the affected CFR title and part. Second, in some cases the proposed rules and final rules in the FR include the relevant CFR subjects. In total there are 826 CFR subjects, though many of these are rarely used.7
We collect all documents in the FR from the Rules and Regulations, Proposed Rules, and Notices sections over the 1994 to 2019 period.8For each of these documents, we download the category of the document (rule, proposed rule, or notice), the agency that submitted the document, and the entire text of the document. For proposed rules and final rules, we additionally collect the CFR citations (title and part affected by the new rule) and the CFR subject.
On average over the sample period, notices represent 44% of the words in the FR, proposed rules 24%, and final rules 32%.9The fact that notices are the largest category is consistent with their being the broadest measure of the government's day-to-day activities. As described above, they include details on the many hearings and meetings.
Figure1shows the upward trend in all three series (notices, proposed rules, and rules) through 2016, followed by a marked drop starting in 2017, the beginning of Donald Trump's presidency. This is consistent with his stated intent of decreasing the role of the government. There are similar (but much smaller) drops each time a new president takes office, for example, in 2001 and 2009, reflecting the fact that it takes time for a new president to exercise his agenda. There also tends to be an increase in the Rules section in the last year of a president's term, for example, 2000, 2008, and 2016, consistent with the natural desire of any outgoing administration to make a last-minute attempt to implement its regulatory agenda.
article.2 Empirical Strategy and Descriptive Sta:
Our overarching goal is to explore how regulatory fragmentation affects underlying firms. Our measure of the fragmentation each firm-year faces is based on several inputs. First, we identify regulatory topics, via textual analysis of the FR. Second, we determine which agencies regulate each topic, where we rely on the fact that each FR document identifies the agency(ies) that wrote the document. Third, we determine the relevance of each topic for each firm-year by comparing the content of the 100 regulatory topics with the content of each firm's 10-K. Each of these steps is discussed in detail in the following subsections.
Our first step is to identify topics in the FR. We employ LDA, which generates a set of topics in which each topic is a cluster of words that tend to appear jointly in the text. The LDA also provides the share of each document that is dedicated to the topic. By relying on a machine to define topics, we mitigate biases that might arise from manually defining topics, for example, through word lists. Chang et al. (2009) show that LDA output corresponds to human ability to detect topics, while also being objective and replicable.10
To carry out the LDA, we must define the number of topics. By way of comparison, Lowry, Michaely, and Volkova (2020) identify 30 topics in firms’ prospectuses and eight topics in the Security and Exchange Commission's comment letters pertaining to these prospectuses. Naturally, the government's activities are much broader and cover a wider range of topics. We therefore chose 100 topics as our baseline. For robustness, we consider both smaller and larger numbers of topics in SectionIV.Gbelow.
Figure2provides topic clouds for four common topics, out of the full set of 100 topics. Casual observation reveals that common words tend to be grouped into one topic. For example, the most salient words in Topic 3 (Panel A) are “special, habitat, population, area, list,” whereas the most salient words in Topic 5 (Panel B) are “rule, exchange, change, commission, proposal.” While the LDA topic clouds clearly correspond to different subjects, it is not immediately apparent what those subjects are. Giving an economic interpretation to the LDA topics is useful because it facilitates interpretation of the empirical patterns observed in the data.11Our methodology, which we describe in greater detail in theInternet Appendix, SectionA1, takes advantage of the CFR subject list.12Returning to the word clouds of Figure2, we label Topic 3 asEndangered species, Topic 5 asSecurities: investment companies, Topic 21 asGrants: education, and Topic 23 asHealth: insurance. Interestingly, LDA analysis reveals that different segments in the FR contain unique information—none of the topics that fall among the top 10 for notices fall among the top 10 for final rules (see, for example, FigureIA.1in theInternet Appendix). This highlights the importance of accounting for all types of government activity and staying abreast of issues communicated through notices.13
Panel A of TableIshows the topics with the lowest and highest fragmentation across agencies. Topics with particularly low fragmentation includeNuclear materialsandAviation safety: inspection, consistent with such topics being relatively focused and having a clear jurisdiction. However, even these topics have fragmentation measures far greater than zero, in the range of 0.34 to 0.38. This would occur if, for example, one agency covers 75% of the topic and a second agency covers 25%, or if one agency covers 78% of the topic and two other agencies cover 11% each. At the upper extreme, topics such asFreedom of informationandGovernment procurement: small businesseshave agency fragmentation measures of approximately 0.95. This is consistent with the general nature of these topics, which are applicable in different contexts and thus are covered by many agencies.
Panel A of Figure3provides a histogram of the fragmentation of each topic-year across agencies. The figure highlights the heterogeneity across topics. Some topics have a fragmentation score less than 0.25, suggesting that regulation of these issues is highly concentrated. However, the distribution is quite skewed, with most topics having relatively high fragmentation scores.
This measure varies within firms over time. Panel B of TableIlists firms with the highest and lowest dispersion of topics, averaged over the sample period. The first takeaway is that all firms have relatively high dispersion of topics, meaning that they must comply with regulatory requirements that span many different topics. The second takeaway is that the firms with the lowest topic dispersion are all financial institutions. Unlike other operating companies, financial institutions do not have substantial portions devoted to nonfinancial lines of business. The firm with the lowest topic dispersion is First Capital—36.2% of the firm's 10-Ks relate to the topicBanks, banking: credit unions, 13.1% toSecurities: income taxes, and 8.3% toIncome taxes: plus other taxesin 2008. Panel B of Figure3provides a histogram that depicts the distribution of this dispersion measure across firm-year observations. The distribution is nearly normal shaped between 0.9 and 0.99, but it also includes a left-hand tail. Approximately 9% of the observations have a topic dispersion score less than 0.90.
Note that we use the label “Regulatory Fragmentation” twice: once to describe the fragmentation of topici$i$across agencies (RegulatoryFragmentationi,t$Regulatory\ Fragmentatio{n_{i,\ t}}$) and once to describe the exposure of firmf$f$to the fragmentation of each topic across agencies (RegulatoryFragmentationf,t$Regulatory\ Fragmentatio{n_{f,t}}$). The difference between these variables is reflected in the subscripts. For our main economic analysis, where we estimate firm-level regressions, we rely on the firm-level variableRegulatoryFragmentationf,t$Regulatory\ Fragmentatio{n_{f,t}}$.14
Panel C of TableIlists firms with the lowest and highest exposures averaged over the sample period. There is a striking contrast between Panels B and C: Firms in the financial industry havelowdispersion of topics buthighexposure to regulatory fragmentation. Put differently, financial companies concentrate on a relatively small number of topics, but those topics tend to be regulated by many federal agencies.15In contrast, firms in the medical industry have high dispersion of topics but low exposure to regulatory fragmentation. These differences highlight the fact that we capture two distinct notions of regulatory burden: the number of topics and the number of agencies that regulate each topic.
Panel C of Figure3provides a histogram that depicts the distribution of firms’ exposure to regulatory fragmentation. This distribution is considerably less skewed than either the fragmentation of each topic across agencies or the dispersion of topics within firms. The distribution is nearly normal, with only a slight left skewness.
In this subsection, we seek to validate our measure. We first show that it is distinct from regulation quantity. Looking at Panel D of TableI, the correlation between regulatory fragmentation and regulation quantity is negative (–0.24). Moreover, the correlation between regulatory fragmentation and the Kalmenovitz (2023) measures of regulation quantity are also negative, as shown inInternet Appendix Table IA1.16
Panels A and B of TableIIreport descriptive statistics. Starting with data from the FR, we find that the average (median) company is mentioned by only 2.3 (2.0) agencies in a given year. Relative to all companies that an agency oversees, this is arguably quite low. Moving to data from 10-Ks (bottom portion of Panel A), the average (median) company mentions 3.5 (3.0) agencies in a given year, with an interquartile range of 2.0 to 5.0. One of the agencies that is nearly always mentioned is the SEC. In sum, both of these measures contain relevant information, but each measure is almost certainly an incomplete measure of the regulatory oversight faced by companies.

Panel C explores the overlap between the two measures. For each of the 14,592 company-years with at least one mention in the FR, we determine which agencies mention the company in that year, and whether the firm mentions all those agencies in its 10-K. We find that the percent of overlap is only 23.6%. From the other perspective, the overlap is even smaller: 14,214 firm-year observations mention one or more agencies in their 10-K, but all of these agencies cite the company (in FR documents) in only 5.0% of cases.
An advantage of these measures is that they capture salient cases of regulation: Companies mention agencies that are highly relevant to their business models, and agencies mention companies that are the subject of specific regulatory actions.17As such, we expect the measures to be correlated with our primary measure of regulatory fragmentation. We test this conjecture by estimating a set of regressions that are reported in Panel D of TableII. The dependent variable is the dispersion measure based on FR or 10-K (equations (6) and (7) above), and the main independent variable is our primary measure:RegulatoryFragmentation$Regulatory\ Fragmentation$. To be clear, we do not seek to establish a causal relation here but are interested in whether the measures of fragmentation are reasonably correlated. We include company, year, and industry-year fixed effects, cluster standard errors by industry, and normalize all variables such that each coefficient indicates the effect of a one-standard-deviation change. We find that the first measure, which captures the extent to which multiple agencies mention a company in a given year, is significantly positively correlated withRegulatoryFragmentation$Regulatory\ Fragmentation$: A one-standard-deviation increase in fragmentation is associated with a 5.0% increase in the dispersion of mentions in the FR. The second measure, which captures how many agencies are mentioned by the company in its 10-K disclosure form, has a similar positive correlation withRegulatoryFragmentation$Regulatory\ Fragmentation$but is not significant at conventional levels.18
In sum, this analysis validates our measure of regulatory fragmentation. We find that companies with high values ofRegulatoryFragmentation$Regulatory\ Fragmentation$are “fragmented” along both dimensions: They are mentioned by more agencies (in the FR) and they mention more agencies (in the 10-K).
article.3 The Evolution of Government Activity o:
Figure4illustrates trends in firms’ exposures to regulatory fragmentation. In Panel A, we compute the annual average of all firm-year observations within each Fama-French 12 industry. Overall, there is a downward trend in the fragmentation faced by firms. One notable exception is the period 2008 to 2010, when a temporary but large shift increased fragmentation by almost 7 percentage points. After 2010, that trend reversed, and we again see a decline within each industry. In subsequent analysis, we find that this spike is driven by changes in the distribution of regulatory activity within several finance-related topics. The topicIncome taxes: plus other taxeshas the greatest effect on the increase in the period leading up to the crisis; during this time regulatory activity within this topic became less concentrated in Treasury, that is, it became more fragmented. Analogously, the marked decline after 2010 is driven largely by a decrease in fragmentation of two topics:Securities: income taxesandIncome taxes: focus on financial instruments. On average across all firm-years, these two topics are among the top 5 in terms of average weight in the 10-K, and the degree of fragmentation decreased from 0.67 to 0.47 and from 0.78 to 0.60, respectively.
Panel B highlights the extent to which firms within an industry experience both different levels and different year-on-year changes in exposure to fragmentation. For brevity, we focus on the nondurables industry. Strikingly, the variation within an industry (Panel B) is greater than the variation across industries (Panel A).
In theInternet Appendix(SectionII), we investigate how the magnitude of government activity has varied over time and especially around two key national events: the Great Financial Crisis and the 2016 Presidential election. For example, around the Great Financial Crisis, the activity of the SEC, the Federal Reserve System, and the Commodities Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) increased by 18%, 130%, and 520%, respectively. Figure5shows that those dramatic changes affected not only the amount of activity but also its distribution across agencies. Looking first at Panel A, following the Trump election, two of the topics with the greatest increases in fragmentation relate to the environment:Energy conservationandEndangered species. This result is consistent with the primary agencies responsible for these issues receiving less funding, and thus remaining oversight being spread more widely across many agencies. In contrast, two of the topics with the greatest decrease in fragmentation relate to the financial sector:BanksandIncome & other taxes. Indeed, the Trump administration set a goal of reducing the fragmentation of financial regulation, as described in a 2016 GAO report.19Panel B shows the topics with the biggest changes around the financial crisis, which coincides with the election of Barack Obama. Interestingly, some of the topics with the largest increases in fragmentation in Panel A see the largest decreases in Panel B, and vice versa. For example, whereas the fragmentation ofEnergy conservationincreased following the Trump election, it decreased in the 2009 to 2011 period (relative to the prior three-year period).
To provide additional insight into the drivers of the time-series variation in fragmentation, we present a case study focusing on the decrease in the fragmentation of theBankstopic (which was highlighted in Figure5). As shown in Panel A of Figure6, the decrease is driven by two dynamics. First, the SEC is the primary regulator in theBankstopic, and the SEC's activity in this space increased from 60,061 words in 2016 to 88,262 words in 2017. Second, at the same time, the activity of other regulators decreased: Treasury decreased from 7,940 to 7,161 words, the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) decreased from 9,249 to 2,933 words, CFTC decreased from 14,244 to 1,986 words, etc. These general patterns of the SEC becoming more dominant and other agencies decreasing activity are also observed in 2018. Panel B of Figure6shows the regulatory fragmentation, measured each year from 2014 to 2019, for the 12 firms with the highest weight in theBankstopic. Consistent with the increased concentration of banking regulation in the hands of the SEC in 2017, the regulatory fragmentation score for all 12 of these firms decreased markedly.
article.4 Effects of Regulatory Fragmentation on:
In this section, we test our main hypotheses. Our first hypothesis posits that firms incur greater costs when regulatory topics fall under the purview of a larger number of regulatory agencies. First, firms have more uncertainty regarding which regulator they must satisfy. Related, firms may have to satisfy multiple regulators on certain issues. Among firms that engage in lobbying, there is less ability to focus efforts on the most important regulatory agency. This potentially contributes to less information flow regarding regulatory initiatives (see, for example, Blanes i Vidal, Draca, and Fons-Rosen (2012), Bertrand, Bombardini, and Trebbi (2014)) and to regulations that are more detrimental for the firm.20Alternatively, it is possible that regulatory fragmentation is beneficial to firms. When multiple regulators overlap, companies may be able to select their regulator. They may select a regulator based on a perceived ability to better influence new regulation to their advantage. Alternatively, they may select the regulator that offers the least restrictive regulations. Either of these scenarios arguably benefits firms via a race to the bottom in regulation.
Before presenting our main empirical tests, in TableIII, we provide descriptive statistics on average firm characteristics. We categorize the entire sample of firm-years into quintiles based on their levels of topic dispersion (Panel A) and their exposure to regulatory fragmentation (Panel B). For brevity, throughout our firm-level analyses, we refer to firms’ exposure to regulatory fragmentation simply as regulatory fragmentation. All continuous variables are winsorized at the 0.5% and 99.5% values.
Several features are worth highlighting. First, regulatory fragmentation is relatively constant across the topic dispersion quintiles, indicating that the two are distinct phenomena. Second, neither topic dispersion nor regulatory fragmentation is strongly related to firm size, as measured by sales. Third, SG&A expenses are positively related to both measures, providing preliminary evidence that both regulatory fragmentation and topic dispersion are associated with higher costs. Fourth, firm growth (as proxied by sales growth, asset growth, or Tobin's Q) is positively related to topic dispersion but negatively related to regulatory fragmentation. Firms with operations that pertain to a greater number of topics tend to grow faster, but when these topics relate to more distinct regulatory bodies the growth is slower. Scatter bin plots inInternet Appendix Figure IA4lead to similar inferences.
We begin in TableIVby examining the relation between regulatory fragmentation faced by firms and SG&A expenses, as a fraction of assets. Our base specification is shown in column (1). We find that SG&A expenses are positively related to regulatory fragmentation: a one-standard-deviation increase in exposure toregulatory fragmentationis associated with a 4.3% standard-deviation increase inSG&A/assets. When the topic areas in which the firm operates become more fragmented across regulatory agencies, firm costs increase. Results are similar when we replace year fixed effects with year×$ \times $industry fixed effects, as shown in column (4). Here, we compare companies that operate within the same industry boundaries, thus removing industry factors that affect both the regulatory environment and company-level costs.
Findings in the prior section provide initial evidence consistent with the costs of regulatory fragmentation. In this subsection, we provide further evidence by examining the link between exposure toregulatory fragmentationand both firm productivity (TFP) and profitability (ROA).TFPcaptures firm output per unit of input. If greater fragmentation increases required inputs (i.e., more employees or more capital) per unit of output, then we will observe a negative relation betweenfragmentationandTFP. This would be the case if, for example, greater fragmentation requires the firm to expend more resources to satisfy regulatory requirements, thus taking inputs away from more productive uses.
We measure firmTFPfollowing Imrohoroglu and Tuzel (2014) and Olley and Pakes (1996).21Broadly speaking,TFPis a function of three components: firm-level value-added (sales minus nonlabor expenses), the stock of labor (number of employees), and the stock of capital (gross PP&E). The model is estimated using a semiparametric procedure, which relative to ordinary least squares has the advantages of controlling for selection biases, controlling for simultaneity biases, and accounting for firm serial correlation.
Columns (2) and (5) in TableIVreport regressions similar to those in columns (1) and (4). We regressTFPonregulatory fragmentation,regulation quantity, firmtopic dispersion, and relevant controls and fixed effects. The results provide further evidence consistent with the costs of regulatory fragmentation. Across both specifications, we find a significant negative relation betweenfragmentationandTFP. Our strictest specification, which includes company and industry×$ \times $year fixed effects, indicates that a one-standard-deviation increase inregulatoryfragmentationis associated with a 3.6% standard deviation decrease inTFPthe following year. In sum, when regulatory activity becomes fragmented across a greater number of government agencies, firm productivity falls.
The finding that higherregulatory fragmentationleads to higher overhead costs (in terms ofSG&A) and lower productivity (in terms ofTFP) suggests that firms will also observe lower profits.22Columns (3) and (6) provide evidence consistent with this prediction. A one-standard-deviation increase inregulatory fragmentationis associated with a decrease in the following year'sROAthat ranges from 5.3% to 5.9% of a standard deviation.
Somewhat puzzlingly, the coefficient onregulation quantityis insignificant or has the opposite sign as one might expect in all specifications in TableIV. Two main factors contribute to this result. First, our measure ofregulation quantityis highly correlated with thelength of the 10-K(correlation = 0.50). As shown in Panel A of TableIAIIIin theInternet Appendix, when we omit thelength of the 10-Kas a control variable, the coefficient onregulation quantity, which is significantly positively related toROAin TableIV, becomes significantly negative. Second, Panels B and C of TableIAIIIshow that the effects of regulation, in terms of both fragmentation and quantity, are greater among small firms than large firms.23For instance, a one-standard-deviation increase inregulation fragmentationis associated with a 4.4% standard deviation decrease inROAamong large firms versus a 7.8% effect among small firms. Similarly, a one-standard-deviation increase inregulation quantitydecreasesROAby 3.5% among large firms and 10.2% among small firms, and the effect is statistically significant.
The finding that regulatory fragmentation is related to both higher costs and lower productivity suggests that it will negatively impact the NPV of projects, contributing to lower growth. We examine this conjecture in TableV, which has a format similar as TableIV. Consistent with expectations, we find significantly negative effects of fragmentation on bothsales growthandasset growth. Column (1) indicates that a one-standard-deviation increase in theregulatory fragmentationfaced by the firm in yeartis followed by a 9.9% standard-deviation decrease in the firm'ssales growththe following year. Columns (2) and (5), which useasset growthas the dependent variable, show similar effects. Focusing on column (5), which includes both firm and industry × year fixed effects, a one-standard-deviation increase inregulatory fragmentationis followed by a 14.3% standard deviation decrease in firmasset growthin the following year. We also find thatregulation quantityhas a significant negative effect on bothsales growthandasset growth.
Regulatory
Fragmentation
Dispersion of Topics
within Firm
It is worth highlighting that, similar to the findings forSG&A, profitability, and TFP, the significant negative relation between fragmentation and firm growth occurs both within-firm and within-industry-year. If a firm faces an increase in regulatory fragmentation in yeart$t$, that firm tends to grow slower in the following year.
In this subsection, we consider the channels underlying the above relations. We focus on a key input in firm production, namely, labor. On the one hand, slower growth can cause firms to hire fewer new employees. This suggests a negative relation between regulatory fragmentation and employment. However, regulatory fragmentation may lead firms to hire additional people to satisfy an increase in regulatory requirements (Simkovic et al. (2023)).
Column (6) in TableVprovides evidence of a positive net effect, that is, greater regulatory fragmentation leads firms to hire more employees. Higher labor costs could contribute, in turn, to increased overhead, lower productivity, and decreased profitability, and these results are consistent with findings in TableIV.
While most regulation-related studies focus solely on newly implemented rules, the total regulatory burden faced by a firm stems from multiple factors: one-time costs associated with complying with new rules, ongoing requirements associated with existing rules, efforts related to new potential regulations, and other costs not directly tied to any specific rule. Each of these factors potentially contributes to fragmentation-related costs.
To test the influence of different aspects of the regulatory agenda, we take advantage of the structure of the FR: While rules show only when they are being developed, notices contain information pertaining to both the development of future rules and the implementation of existing regulations.24Panel A of TableVIreports results of regressions similar to those in TablesIVandV, except we calculateregulatory fragmentationseparately across notices versus final rules. The results highlight the significant effects of each stage of regulatory activity. While notices are largely ignored in regulation studies,regulatory fragmentationwithin this stage predicts higher overhead costs, lower productivity, and lower profitability and growth. In contrast, the effects ofregulatory fragmentationon new hiring are concentrated within new rulemaking, consistent with firms hiring more employees to satisfy new regulatory requirements.
We next contrast the impact of new rule making versus rule modifications. We take a unique approach to separating these types of regulatory activity. Rules and proposed rules are assigned a Regulation Identifier Number (RIN), and all modifications to a previous rule are filed under the same RIN. We classify the first document with a given RIN as a new rule and all follow-on documents with the same RIN as modifications. We separately calculate regulatory fragmentationacross new rules (new RIN) and modifications (existing RIN). The correlation between these measures is 0.54, indicating that there is overlap but they also capture different dynamics. When we estimate our regressions, the results reported in Panel B of TableVIshow that both types of regulatory activity are significantly related to firm outcomes, and in many cases the economic magnitudes are similar.
Finally, the regulatory requirements faced by firms are a function of both new and past regulatory activity. Our flow-based measure of fragmentation weights new activity the highest, and it includes a subset of past regulatory activity, in particular, that which is reflected in notices (e.g., revised requirements, filing details). To the extent that the costs of satisfying regulations are highest for new regulations, when the firm must develop new systems and processes, we would expect this flow-based measure to have the greatest predictive power for contemporaneous or one-year-ahead outcomes, and to dissipate over time. Regressions reported inInternet Appendix Table IAVsupport this prediction. To more fully incorporate the effects of past regulatory activity, we form an alternative stock-based measure of fragmentation based on the weighted average of the past five years of regulatory activity (as represented by the FR), where more recent years are weighted more heavily. As shown in Panel C of TableVI, when we estimate our baseline regressions with the new measure, the results are similar to our main results.25
A potential concern is that our results reflect changes in firm operations, rather than changes in the fragmentation of topics across agencies. We conduct two set of tests to verify that this is not the case.26We first exclude firm-years with a substantial change in operations, as the bias should be greatest in these cases. We use three measures for such operational changes: (i) We exclude firms that changed their industry affiliation using a fine measure of 500 industry groups (Hoberg and Phillips (2010,2016)), (ii) we exclude firms whose number of operating segments changed, (iii) we exclude firms whose assets changed (in absolute terms) by more than 20%. For each alternative sample, we estimate our baseline regressions withSG&A,TFP, andsales growth. The results, summarized in TableVII, are qualitatively similar to those of our main analyses.27
Regulatory
Fragmentation
Regulatory
Fragmentation
Regulatory
Fragmentation
Second, we estimate placebo tests in which we randomly match topics in each firm's 10-K with topics in the FR. We repeat the process 1,000 times and summarize thet-statistics on fragmentation in histograms. If our main results were driven by changes in firm operations and associated changes in the set of relevant firm topics (rather than by changes in the fragmentation of each topic), then we would obtain similar significance in these placebo tests. However, as shown in Figure7, the findings reject this possibility. For example, forSG&A, the placebot-statistic falls below the actualt-statistic in 945 out of 1,000 iterations.28
Additionally, to test for potential bias due to correlated omitted variables, we evaluate coefficient stability using the methodology of Oster (2019). As shown inInternet Appendix Table IA9, the coefficients on regulatory fragmentation are relatively stable when we omit the control variables. Finally, the results are also robust to recalculating our main measure of regulatory fragmentation using different sections of the 10-K (Internet Appendix Table IAX) and different numbers of topics (Internet Appendix Table IAXI).
Classic economic theories argue that regulation can raise barriers to entry (see, for example, Stigler (1971)). We conjecture that the fragmentation of regulation has a similar effect. Specifically, we predict that fragmentation deters entry and increases exit, particularly among smaller firms for whom the fixed costs associated with this regulatory burden are greater. To capture the effects of regulatory burden among firms competing in the same product market, we use the product market similarity scores of Hoberg and Phillips (2016). For each focal firm, the published TNIC-3 database lists peer firms with relatively high similarity scores.29
Our analysis is summarized in TableVIII. We use the workhorse specification (equation (8)) where the main independent variable of interest isregulatory fragmentation, but now the dependent variable captures various aspects of the focal firm's competitive environment. We first focus on entry—the number of new peers that went public during yeart+1$t + 1$(column (1)), and the number of peers that were already public in yeart$t$but moved into the focal firm's industry in yeart+1$t + 1$(column (2)). We find that fragmentation is significantly negatively related to both measures. In economic terms, a one-standard-deviation increase infragmentationis associated with a 4.9% standard deviation decrease in thenumber of initial public offeringsin the focal industry.
Regulatory
Fragmentation
Next, we shift focus to exits. We compute the percentage of peers in yeart$t$that continue to trade in yeart+1$t + 1$but are no longer peers. Column (3) focuses on exit by small firms and column (4) on exit by large firms, defined based on average assets within Fama-French 48 industry. Our findings are consistent with fragmentation creating barriers to entry that hurt small firms but benefit larger firms: a one-standard-deviation increase infragmentationis associated with a 3.5% standard deviation increase in the number of small peers that exit the focal firm's industry, but a 4.1% standard deviation decrease in the number of large peers that exit.
Finally, column (5) examines the net effect of fragmentation on industry size. The dependent variable is the number of peers in yeart+1$t + 1$. Consistent with the significantly lower entry and higher exit shown in columns (1) to (3), we find a significant negative effect on industry size: A one-standard-deviation increase in fragmentation is associated with 6.1 fewer peers in the industry. In sum, the results throughout this section demonstrate that the costly nature of fragmentation deters entry and contributes to exit in ways that negatively affect membership in the industry.
article.5 Sources of Burden Arising from Regulat:
Results so far show that regulatory fragmentation is costly for firms. In this section, we seek to provide evidence on the sources of the burden. Specifically, we evaluate regulatory duplication, regulatory inconsistency, and decreased regulatory capture.
If multiple agencies regulate a given topic, regulatory duplication refers to redundancy in requirements, for example, if multiple agencies require that firms fill out forms that track pollution levels. Duplication could be helpful if it helps ensure that the underlying regulatory requirement is fulfilled, but the redundancy can be costly for companies. In contrast, regulatory inconsistency (see Agarwal et al. (2014)) means differences in requirements across agencies, for example, if agency X uses the metric system while agency Y uses the imperial system. Inconsistency in requirements could increase the costs of compliance. Moreover, inconsistency could create uncertainty in how to resolve discrepancies across requirements.
The duplication and inconsistency are not mutually exclusive, and both could contribute to higher costs and lower efficiency, profits, and growth. To empirically evaluate their importance, we rely on the fact that some documents in the FR are co-authored by multiple agencies while others are authored by a single agency.30When agencies collaborate, it is unlikely that the resulting regulation is riddled with inconsistencies. Thus, co-authored regulations should have lower adverse effects (in absolute value). To test this idea, we augment our baseline specification with a variable that represents the firm's exposure to co-authored topics. We construct the variable in three steps. First, we construct a topic-year measure by calculating the percent of words within each topic that come from co-authored documents. Second, we bring this measure to the firm-year level by calculating the weighted average across topics: Weights are the percent of the firm's 10-K devoted to each topic and are then multiplied by a dummy equal to one if the respective topic's percent of co-authored words is above median and zero otherwise. Third, we identify firms with high exposure to co-authored topics. For ease of interpretation, we define the variableCoAuthoredf,t$CoAuthore{d_{f,t}}$as a dummy variable which equal to one if this weighted average is above median and zero otherwise.
Our independent variable of interest isCoAuthoredf,t$CoAuthore{d_{f,t}}$interacted with our primary measure of regulatory fragmentation. We use a fully interacted model, whereCoAuthoredf,t$CoAuthore{d_{f,t}}$is interacted with each of the independent variables. The results are reported in TableIX. Following earlier specifications, dependent variables include measures of costs, productivity, profitability, growth, and employment. Consistent with our predictions, the coefficient on the interaction termCoAuthoredf,t·RegulatoryFragmentationf,t$CoAuthore{d_{f,t}} \cdot RegulatoryFragmentatio{n_{f,t}}$runs in the opposite directions of the coefficient onRegulatoryFragmentation$RegulatoryFragmentation$in nearly all cases. For example, usingSGAas the dependent variable, the average effect of fragmentation is6.0%$6.0\% $but the effect for firms that are more sensitive to “co-authored” topics is only4.6%$4.6\% $.

×$ \times $Coauthored
In sum, the results indicate that the negative effects of fragmentation are less pronounced when agencies collaborate, that is, when the costs of fragmentation only reflect duplicative redundancy. In contrast, the negative effects of fragmentation are stronger when agencies independently regulate the same topic, suggesting that the resulting rules are more likely to suffer from inconsistency (in addition to potential redundancy).
Regulatory capture refers to the ability of firms to influence regulators through lobbying and other channels. On the one hand, increased fragmentation may decrease regulatory capture; when multiple agencies oversee a regulatory issue, firms have less clarity on where to direct their influence efforts. Moreover, the impact of efforts to promote or block a given regulatory action is likely reduced. On the other hand, because regulatory fragmentation adversely affects firms, an increase in fragmentation may lead firms to increase their capture activities in an attempt to counteract these effects.
Using lobbying as a proxy for firm resources devoted to capturing regulators, we estimate regressions similar to those in TablesIVandV.31The results are reported in TableX. The dependent variable equals thelog of lobbying expendituresin columns (1) and (3), and rawlobbying expendituresin columns (2) and (4) (in USD millions). The independent variable of interest isregulatory fragmentation. In addition to the control variables used in prior regressions, columns (1) and (2) include company and year fixed effects, and columns (3) and (4) additionally include industry×$ \times $year fixed effects. Across all specifications, greaterregulatory fragmentationis associated with a significant decrease in firm lobbying. These results are consistent with firms concluding that the benefits of lobbying are lower when it is less clear where to direct lobbying efforts.

article.6 Determinants of Regulatory Fragmentati:
The costly nature of fragmentation raises the question of what factors contribute to this regulatory structure. We posit three such factors. First, agency costs among regulators may lead to fragmentation. As formalized in “tollbooth models” (De Soto (1990), Shleifer and Vishny (1998)), a regulator can extract rents by writing additional regulations. Rents may include internal promotions or private sector jobs (Glaeser, Johnson, and Shleifer (2001)). For example, regulatory schooling theories argue that regulators use their tenure in the government to accumulate human capital and increase the value of their outside option (e.g., Bond and Glode (2014)). Rents may also include opportunities to expand the agency (empire building), and in extreme cases, selling government property for personal gain (as in Shleifer and Vishny (1993)). Note that tollbooth models are closely related to theories of public choice (e.g., Tullock (1967)) and regulatory capture (e.g., Stigler (1971)), which suggest that regulation reduces social welfare by creating rent opportunities.32Second, fragmentation may result from efforts to serve the public interest (Pigou (1938)). For example, agency X may duplicate agency Y's requirement to ensure compliance, that is, to mitigate attempts to subvert regulation (Glaeser and Shleifer (2003)). Alternatively, agency X may impose a requirement that is inconsistent with agency Y if it determines that Y's regulation is insufficient or misguided. Third, unintentional actions among regulators may play a role in fragmentation. The federal government is large, with more than one million employees spread across many agencies, and actors within one agency may be unaware of regulation coming out of other agencies.
The results in TableIXindicate that unintentional actions cannot alone explain fragmentation and its negative effects, although it may have some influence. Even when fragmentation is based on joint actions by government agencies, it is associated with significantly higher costs and significantly lower productivity, profits, and growth.
To investigate agency-type issues, we obtain compensation and employee rank data (via Freedom of Information Act requests) for individuals working in 75 government agencies in our sample.33We conjecture that the prospects of an internal promotion could motivate federal employees to increase their rulemaking docket and initiate more actions based on existing rules (similar to Kalmenovitz (2021)).34Since promotions are influenced by employee demographics, and these demographics plausibly vary across agencies, we first estimate a model of promotion at the employee-year level as a function of an individual's tenure at the agency. We also includeagency × rankandoccupationfixed effects given the importance of occupation and hierarchy in promotion decisions. We average the residuals at the agency-year level to obtain a measure of unexplained promotion activity net of observable employee characteristics. The resulting measure is the dependent variable, measured in yeart+1. Independent variables include the number of words written by the agency in yeartin the two primary sections of the FR—notices and rules. Notices have the advantage of being a timely measure of effort. Rules, in contrast, typically represent the accumulation of many years’ effort and thus may be less precisely measured. All regressions include year and agency fixed effects.
The results are reported in TableXI. Across all columns, we find a significantly positive relation between promotions and agency activity. This indicates that employees are rewarded for increasing rulemaking and other regulatory activities. However, adding FR documents does not necessarily increase regulatory fragmentation as this depends on the topic distribution of other agencies. Accordingly, we investigate whether employees are especially rewarded for venturing into new topics that are outside the agency's core expertise. All else equal, such activities would contribute to overall fragmentation.
One limit to an in-depth analysis on this issue is that we only have data at the agency-year level. That is, we are unable to identify the rules and notices that each individual employee works on. With this limitation in mind, we conduct the following agency-year-level analysis: We compute the percent of words that belong to the agency's top 10 topics, and we add the interaction between this variable,WordsCore Topic, and the total number of words written by the agency.35If agents are rewarded for focusing on the agency's core areas, then we should find a positive coefficient on the interaction term. However, coefficients on these interaction terms are negative and weakly significant. In sum, we find no evidence that agents are rewarded for concentrating on the agency's main area, and weak evidence that they are rewarded for extending effort into other areas in ways that contribute positively to fragmentation.
article.7 Conclusion:
The federal government is the largest economic agent in the country, and as such the government's activities have a tremendous impact on companies. Yet, studies of the impact of government activities tend to focus on small subsets of such activities, particularly on various federal regulations that are already in place. In this paper, we choose a different approach and develop the first rigorous measure of the entirety of the federal government's actions. We do so by analyzing the voluminous official documents published daily by the government in the FR.
We use a machine learning algorithm to categorize all FR activities into 100 topics and label each topic according to its economic focus. We find that topics are often overseen simultaneously by multiple federal agencies, with substantial shifts occurring around major macroeconomic events. We combine these topic areas with information from company 10-Ks to construct a firm-level measure of regulatory fragmentation. This measure is based on the intuition that each company is subject to multiple regulatory topics (calculated based on its 10-K filing), and each topic is regulated by multiple agencies (calculated based on FR documents). It is a firm-specific, time-varying measure, with higher fragmentation indicating that the company's businesses are overseen by a larger number of federal agencies.
We find that regulatory fragmentation is associated with significantly higher SG&A costs, lower productivity (TFP), lower profitability, and slower growth. These results are conditional on other measures of regulatory burden and complexity, which we derive separately, and are identified both within-firm and within industry×$ \times $year. Our findings indicate that companies internalize the costs of having to keep abreast of multiple regulatory bodies. When a firm must answer to a larger number of government agencies, it diverts resources away from value-increasing efforts. In sum, companies benefit if their businesses are regulated by fewer government agencies: They face less uncertainty, and they find it easier to capture the relevant regulators.
Overall, our paper is related to the emerging literature that studies how regulation in all of its forms affects economic activity. Our empirical approach applies advanced linguistic tools to a long-established but little-used administrative data set, namely, the FR. This approach allows us to study broad trends in the government's activities and to develop micro-level evidence on how individual firms respond to changes in those activities. Finally, our focus on regulatory fragmentation sheds light on a less-understood aspect of regulation, namely, the “industrial organization” of the federal government, whereby multiple agencies regulate similar topics. This opens the door for future work on the effects of fragmentation, for example, fragmentation in the implementation of regulations via enforcement actions.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
article.Appendix A: Agency Names: This table lists the full names and common abbreviations of federal agencies that are frequently mentioned in the paper.
article.Appendix B: Variable Definitions: Note that we use the label “Regulatory fragmentation” twice: first to describe the dispersion of topici$i$across agencies (denotedRegulatoryFragmentationi,t$Regulatory\ Fragmentatio{n_{i,\ t}}$), and second to describe the exposure of firmf$f$to the fragmentation of each topic across agencies (denotedRegulatoryFragmentationf,t$Regulatory\ Fragmentatio{n_{{\mathrm{f}},t}}$). We also estimate regulatory fragmentation separately across subsets of documents. For example, we estimate it separately for notices, proposed rules, and rules. We first estimate agency dispersion for notices (proposed rules/rules) only using the formula from the table below and applying it only to notices (proposed rules/rules). We then use this new measure of agency dispersion and reestimate regulatory fragmentation using the same formula as before. We use a similar approach to estimate regulatory fragmentation across new versus modified rules.

AItitle: 监管碎片化侵蚀企业全要素生产率：联邦公报文本分析揭示政策摩擦成本
AIsummary: 【监管碎片化：当政府"多头管理"拖累企业效率】  这篇经济学论文首次系统研究了美国联邦政府"监管碎片化"现象——即多个机构交叉监管同一议题对企业的影响。传统研究多聚焦单一法规的合规成本，而作者创新性地通过机器学习分析1994-2019年《联邦公报》百万级文本数据，结合企业年报，构建了动态量化指标：他们将监管内容分解为100个主题（如住房补贴、证券税收），计算每个主题涉及的机构数量，再根据企业年报关键词匹配其面临的监管复杂度。  研究发现，监管碎片化使企业行政成本增加13%（中位数），全要素生产率下降3.6%，更导致小企业退出率上升。通过对比机构联合发文与独立监管案例，揭示核心矛盾并非简单重复监管，而是机构间规则冲突带来的合规不确定性。有趣的是，监管碎片化反而降低了企业游说支出——当监管权分散时，企业甚至不知道应该"公关"哪个部门。  该研究突破性地将自然语言处理引入监管经济学，为量化政府行为影响提供了新范式。数据显示，特朗普执政期间金融监管集中化使相关企业成本下降7%，而环保监管碎片化则使能源企业效率受损。这为优化监管架构提供了数据支撑，也揭示了官僚体系扩张背后的激励机制：监管者撰写更多文件与其晋升概率正相关。
Highlights:
学术要点 1：监管碎片化（Regulatory Fragmentation） 指同一监管议题被多个联邦机构共同监管的现象，通过联邦公报文本的潜在狄利克雷分析（LDA）量化，体现为机构间监管权的分散程度，导致企业合规成本增加和效率下降。|||
学术要点 2：机构协调成本（Agency Coordination Costs） 监管机构间因缺乏协同产生的隐性成本，表现为独立发文（solo-authored documents）与联合发文（co-authored documents）对合规影响的差异，独立监管导致更高程度的不一致性负担。|||
学术要点 3：主题分散度（Topic Dispersion） 企业年报中监管话题分布的熵值测度，反映企业面临的跨领域监管复杂度，与机构碎片化形成正交维度，共同构成监管负担的完整框架。|||
学术要点 4：动态规则流分析（Dynamic Rule Flow Analysis） 基于联邦公报不同阶段（通知/提案/终稿）的监管活动追踪，揭示早期规则制定阶段（notices）对企业的实质性影响，突破传统研究仅关注终稿规则的局限。|||
学术要点 5：监管捕获抑制效应（Regulatory Capture Deterrence） 碎片化环境下企业游说效率下降的现象，多机构监管导致企业难以聚焦游说目标，降低监管捕获可能性，形成制度性约束机制。
